news_summary: Recent breakthroughs in Chinese sodium battery technology rival Tesla standards, the debut of precise nuclear clocks promises advanced timekeeping, and new research reveals T. rex's prolonged growth period.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées de la technologie chinoise des batteries au sodium rivalisent avec les normes de Tesla, le lancement d'horloges nucléaires ultraprécises promet une mesure du temps de pointe, et de nouvelles recherches révèlent que la période de croissance du T. rex était plus longue que prévu.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en la tecnología china de baterías de sodio rivalizan con los estándares de Tesla; la presentación de relojes nucleares de alta precisión promete una medición del tiempo más avanzada; y una nueva investigación revela que el T. rex tuvo un periodo de crecimiento prolongado.

articles.title: Chinese sodium battery surprised scientists by matching key Tesla benchmarks
articles.summary: Researchers found that a Chinese sodium-ion battery performs far better than expected, with production quality and design features comparable to Tesla’s batteries. If engineers can improve cold-weather charging and energy density, sodium could become a cheaper and more abundant alternative to lithium for EVs and large-scale energy storage.
articles.content:
A widely used sodium-ion battery developed by Chinese manufacturer Hina has achieved performance and manufacturing quality levels comparable to Tesla's lithium-ion batteries, according to research published in the Cell Press journal Cell Reports Physical Science.

The findings suggest sodium-ion technology could become a lower-cost alternative for future electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems. To reach that goal, however, the battery will need further improvements in low-temperature charging and energy density. Unlike lithium, sodium is abundant and readily available, making it an attractive material for reducing battery costs and supply chain concerns.

"The combination of good uniformity, high power capability, and strong low-temperature performance makes these cells attractive for stationary storage, grid services, and shorter-range or commercial vehicles where potential lower cost and resource availability matter more than maximum driving range," says Moritz Schütte, a battery researcher at RWTH Aachen University in Germany.

Comparing Sodium-Ion Batteries With Tesla Technology

To evaluate the Hina battery, Schütte and colleagues examined 120 sodium-ion cells using impedance spectroscopy, a non-destructive method that measures battery uniformity.

The researchers then tested the cells under a variety of real-world operating conditions. Performance was measured across different current levels and temperatures ranging from −20 °C to 45 °C. The team also used X-rays to examine the batteries internally before disassembling them to analyze electrode dimensions, material composition, and microscopic structural features.

One notable discovery was the battery's tabless, double-aluminum current collector design. This configuration helps reduce electrical resistance and promotes more even temperature distribution throughout the cell. The researchers noted that this design closely resembles the architecture currently used in Tesla batteries.

"We were positively surprised by how uniform the cells are," says Schütte.

Strengths and Remaining Challenges

Despite the encouraging results, the researchers identified several areas where the sodium-ion battery still trails leading lithium-ion technologies.

"The high-power performance was better than one might expect from an early commercial sodium-ion product," says Schütte. "However, for applications that require frequent charging at low ambient temperatures, appropriate thermal management or operating strategies will be important because low-temperature charging remains a clear weakness."

The team also detected unexpectedly high concentrations of copper in certain regions of the battery's cathode. In addition, the copper was unevenly distributed throughout those areas.

According to Schütte, this finding "raises interesting questions about its role in performance and aging."

"It will be exciting to see future sodium-ion technologies that are free of nickel and copper, as well, while achieving competitive energy density," he said.

Why Sodium Could Matter for Future Batteries

Because sodium is far more abundant and widely available than lithium, manufacturers could potentially lower raw material costs while reducing long-term supply chain risks.

Sodium-ion batteries also maintain strong performance under load in cold conditions, making them attractive for stationary energy storage systems and mobile applications operating in colder climates.

"However, today's commercial sodium-ion cells generally have lower energy density than the best lithium-ion cells, and the technology is less mature overall," said Schütte.

Next Steps for Sodium-Ion Research

The researchers plan to focus on improving charging performance at low temperatures, with the goal of enabling safer and more efficient charging below 0°C.

Additional work will also explore ways to optimize the materials used in sodium-ion batteries.

"Advances in hard-carbon anodes and electrolyte formulations may be especially promising," he said.

The study was supported by the Federal Ministry of Research, Technology, and Space and the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in sodium-ion battery performance comparable to Tesla's lithium-ion batteries, with potential for large-scale impact on electric vehicles and energy storage by offering a cheaper, abundant alternative to lithium. The story includes detailed research findings, real-world testing, and outlines future improvements, demonstrating clear substance and broad significance for sustainable energy technology.
articles.personality_title: Chinese sodium battery matches Tesla’s quality, promising cheaper energy storage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been looking for cheaper and more available materials to make batteries for electric vehicles and energy storage. Lithium, used in Tesla batteries, is expensive and not easy to get. Sodium, which is much more common, is one option, but its batteries usually don’t work as well.

**What happened** – Researchers studied a sodium-ion battery made by a Chinese company called Hina. They tested 120 of these batteries in many ways, including at cold and hot temperatures. The tests showed that these sodium batteries perform almost as well as Tesla’s lithium batteries in power and quality. The design of the battery, especially a special part called a “tabless, double-aluminum current collector,” helps it work efficiently and stay cool, similar to Tesla’s design.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because sodium is cheaper and more available than lithium. If these batteries can be improved, especially to charge better in cold weather and hold more energy, they could replace lithium batteries in many uses. This would make electric cars and large energy storage systems less expensive and easier to produce.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on making these sodium batteries charge better in cold temperatures and find better materials to improve their energy storage. They want to make batteries without problematic metals like copper and nickel, which affect performance and battery life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A Chinese sodium-ion battery has shown performance close to Tesla’s lithium batteries, offering a promising path to cheaper and more abundant energy storage solutions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une batterie au sodium chinoise rivalise avec la qualité des batteries Tesla
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des matériaux moins chers et plus disponibles pour fabriquer des batteries pour véhicules électriques et stockage d’énergie. Le lithium, utilisé dans les batteries Tesla, est coûteux et rare. Le sodium, beaucoup plus courant, est une option, mais ses batteries fonctionnent généralement moins bien.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié une batterie sodium-ion fabriquée par une entreprise chinoise appelée Hina. Ils ont testé 120 batteries dans différentes conditions, y compris par temps froid et chaud. Les tests ont montré que ces batteries au sodium fonctionnent presque aussi bien que les batteries lithium de Tesla en puissance et qualité. La conception de la batterie, notamment un composant spécial appelé « collecteur de courant double en aluminium sans onglet », aide à son efficacité et à sa régulation thermique, similaire au design de Tesla.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car le sodium est moins cher et plus disponible que le lithium. Si ces batteries sont améliorées, notamment pour mieux se charger par temps froid et stocker plus d’énergie, elles pourraient remplacer les batteries lithium dans de nombreuses applications. Cela rendrait les voitures électriques et les grands systèmes de stockage moins coûteux et plus faciles à produire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont travailler à améliorer la charge des batteries sodium par temps froid et à trouver de meilleurs matériaux pour augmenter leur capacité énergétique. Ils veulent aussi fabriquer des batteries sans métaux problématiques comme le cuivre et le nickel, qui affectent la performance et la durée de vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une batterie sodium-ion chinoise a montré des performances proches des batteries lithium de Tesla, ouvrant la voie à des solutions de stockage d’énergie moins chères et plus abondantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Batería de sodio china iguala la calidad de Tesla y promete almacenamiento más barato
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan materiales más baratos y disponibles para fabricar baterías para vehículos eléctricos y almacenamiento de energía. El litio, que usan las baterías de Tesla, es caro y difícil de conseguir. El sodio, mucho más común, es una opción, pero sus baterías suelen funcionar peor.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron una batería de iones de sodio fabricada por una empresa china llamada Hina. Probaron 120 baterías en diferentes condiciones, incluyendo temperaturas frías y calientes. Los resultados mostraron que estas baterías de sodio funcionan casi tan bien como las baterías de litio de Tesla en potencia y calidad. El diseño especial de la batería, con un “colector de corriente doble de aluminio sin pestañas”, ayuda a que funcione eficientemente y mantenga una temperatura uniforme, similar al diseño de Tesla.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque el sodio es más barato y está más disponible que el litio. Si estas baterías mejoran, especialmente para cargar mejor en frío y almacenar más energía, podrían reemplazar las baterías de litio en muchas aplicaciones. Esto haría que los autos eléctricos y los sistemas grandes de almacenamiento sean más económicos y fáciles de fabricar.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán para que estas baterías de sodio carguen mejor en temperaturas bajas y para encontrar mejores materiales que aumenten su capacidad energética. También quieren eliminar metales problemáticos como el cobre y el níquel, que afectan el rendimiento y la vida útil.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una batería de sodio china mostró un rendimiento cercano al de las baterías de litio de Tesla, abriendo una vía prometedora para soluciones de almacenamiento de energía más baratas y abundantes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, stylized sodium-ion battery cell with intertwined copper and aluminum currents flowing smoothly inside, set against a softly lit background featuring abstract, crystalline structures symbolizing cold temperatures and energy storage, rendered in warm, natural hues of amber, copper, and soft gray.

articles.title: The first ticking ‘nuclear clocks’ are here — what can they do?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01909-7Two research teams have created a new, long-awaited type of timekeeper.
articles.content:
The clocks use a precise laser to measure transitions inside the nuclei of thorium-229 atoms.Credit: Getty

Two teams of physicists have made the world’s first nuclear clocks. These radical new devices use fluctuations in the energy states of an atom’s nucleus to keep time, rather than those of its electrons, which atomic clocks currently use to define the length of a second.

Working out how to extract the ‘tick’ from a nucleus and use it to keep time has taken more than 20-years. Nuclear clocks should be more robust and portable than the best available clocks today because nuclei are hard to perturb and protected in a crystal. As well as potentially someday being more precise, they also give physicists an unprecedented way to probe the forces at play inside a nucleus.

Two nuclear clocks have been presented in two studies, which were posted on the preprint server arXiv on 3 and 7 June, by teams in Europe1 and China2. They show that nuclear clocks have gone from a system with “potential” to “a functioning precision instrument” that can be used to search for new physics, says Gilad Perez, a theoretical physicist at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Rehovot, Israel.

Creating a nuclear clock is “a dream come true”, says Thorsten Schumm, an atomic physicist at the Vienna University of Technology and a lead member of the European team. Until recently the field had been “a calm niche” to work in, he says. “Now we have a fierce but friendly global competition.”

Tick tock

All clocks require a stable oscillation — like that of a swinging pendulum — to keep time. In the best atomic clocks, this oscillation is created by the visible wavelength of light that triggers an electron to jump between energy levels. Physicists determine the specific frequency of laser light required to trigger this shift in electron state, then use that frequency to keep time.

A nuclear clock is different. Rather than causing electrons to jump between energy levels, it keeps time by boosting the protons and neutrons inside the nucleus of thorium-229 atoms to a higher energy state. Most elements require an enormous amount of energy to reorganize their nuclei, but thorium is unusual because it has stable energy levels that are so close together that just the nudge of ultraviolet laser light can prompt the shift.

‘Nuclear clock’ breakthrough paves the way for super-precise timekeeping

Physicists had suspected thorium’s special properties for decades, but it wasn’t until 2024 that they finally succeeded in triggering the nuclear transition in a millimetre-sized crystal of calcium fluoride loaded with trillions of thorium-229 atoms. Later that year another team pinpointed the precise frequency at which it happens.

The only thing that was missing for a nuclear clock to work was a way to lock the frequency of the laser with the natural timekeeper and keep the clock’s tick speed from drifting over time. Both teams achieved this by monitoring how much the laser light was absorbed by the thorium-229 atoms. When the laser was in the right range the signal’s strength dipped as photons got absorbed, says Schumm. But if the frequency drifts, “you see the signal coming up again and can immediately correct for that”, he says.

The groups differed in their exact methods: the group in China, led by Shiqian Ding, a physicist at Tsinghua University in Beijing, used a laser around 100 times more powerful than the European one, but their crystal had a lower concentration of thorium-229 atoms, so overall the signals produced by both clocks were comparable.

Both teams’ clocks ticked reliably, only drifting over the course of a day by the equivalent of around 1 second in 3 million years (although, for now, that is still below the stability of the best optical atomic clocks, which gain or lose a second every 40 billion years).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in precision timekeeping with the creation of the first nuclear clocks, which represent a major technological advancement. This innovation has broad scientific implications, including more robust and portable clocks and new ways to probe nuclear physics, benefiting the general scientific community and potentially many technological applications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create the first nuclear clocks for ultra-precise timekeeping
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, scientists have used atomic clocks to measure time by watching electrons jump between energy levels in atoms. These clocks are very accurate and help define the length of a second.

**What happened** – Two research teams, one in Europe and one in China, have built the first nuclear clocks. Instead of using electrons, these clocks measure changes inside the nucleus of thorium-229 atoms. They use lasers to make the nucleus jump to a higher energy state and then keep time based on these changes. The teams succeeded by carefully matching their laser frequencies to the natural energy changes in the nucleus.

**Impact** – Nuclear clocks could be stronger and easier to carry than current atomic clocks because nuclei are less affected by outside disturbances. They also offer scientists a new way to study the forces inside an atom’s nucleus. Although these clocks are not yet more stable than the best atomic clocks, they already lose only about one second every three million years, showing great promise.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving nuclear clocks to make them even more stable and precise. This may lead to better navigation systems, more accurate scientific experiments, and new discoveries about how the universe works at a tiny scale.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two teams have built the first nuclear clocks, marking a major step toward more precise and reliable timekeeping using the heart of an atom.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les premiers horloges nucléaires créées pour une mesure du temps ultra-précise
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les scientifiques utilisent des horloges atomiques qui mesurent le temps en observant des électrons sauter entre des niveaux d’énergie dans les atomes. Ces horloges sont très précises et définissent la durée d’une seconde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux équipes de recherche, une en Europe et une en Chine, ont construit les premières horloges nucléaires. Au lieu d’utiliser les électrons, ces horloges mesurent les changements à l’intérieur du noyau des atomes de thorium-229. Elles utilisent des lasers pour exciter le noyau à un niveau d’énergie supérieur et gardent le temps en fonction de ces changements. Les équipes ont réussi en ajustant précisément la fréquence de leurs lasers aux changements d’énergie naturels du noyau.

**Impact** – Les horloges nucléaires pourraient être plus solides et plus faciles à transporter que les horloges atomiques actuelles, car les noyaux sont moins sensibles aux perturbations extérieures. Elles offrent aussi aux scientifiques une nouvelle manière d’étudier les forces à l’intérieur du noyau atomique. Bien que ces horloges ne soient pas encore plus stables que les meilleures horloges atomiques, elles perdent déjà seulement environ une seconde tous les trois millions d’années, ce qui montre un grand potentiel.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les horloges nucléaires pour les rendre encore plus stables et précises. Cela pourrait conduire à de meilleurs systèmes de navigation, des expériences scientifiques plus exactes, et de nouvelles découvertes sur le fonctionnement de l’univers à très petite échelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Deux équipes ont construit les premières horloges nucléaires, marquant une avancée majeure vers une mesure du temps plus précise et fiable en utilisant le cœur de l’atome.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean los primeros relojes nucleares para medir el tiempo con ultra precisión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han usado relojes atómicos para medir el tiempo observando cómo los electrones saltan entre niveles de energía en los átomos. Estos relojes son muy precisos y definen la duración de un segundo.

**Qué pasó** – Dos equipos de investigación, uno en Europa y otro en China, construyeron los primeros relojes nucleares. En lugar de usar electrones, estos relojes miden cambios dentro del núcleo de átomos de torio-229. Usan láseres para hacer que el núcleo suba a un nivel de energía más alto y mantienen el tiempo basándose en esos cambios. Los equipos lograron esto ajustando cuidadosamente la frecuencia de sus láseres a los cambios naturales de energía del núcleo.

**Impacto** – Los relojes nucleares podrían ser más fuertes y fáciles de transportar que los relojes atómicos actuales porque los núcleos son menos afectados por perturbaciones externas. También ofrecen a los científicos una nueva forma de estudiar las fuerzas dentro del núcleo atómico. Aunque estos relojes aún no son más estables que los mejores relojes atómicos, ya pierden solo aproximadamente un segundo cada tres millones de años, mostrando un gran potencial.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando los relojes nucleares para hacerlos aún más estables y precisos. Esto podría conducir a mejores sistemas de navegación, experimentos científicos más exactos y nuevos descubrimientos sobre cómo funciona el universo a escala muy pequeña.

**Resumen en una frase** – Dos equipos han construido los primeros relojes nucleares, marcando un paso importante hacia una medición del tiempo más precisa y confiable usando el corazón del átomo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, crystalline structure symbolizing a calcium fluoride crystal embedded with tiny, radiant orbs representing thorium-229 nuclei, illuminated softly by a delicate ultraviolet laser beam that pulses rhythmically like a heartbeat, set against a warm, abstract background of gentle, natural hues to evoke precision and the groundbreaking advancement of nuclear timekeeping.

articles.title: T. rex took 40 years to reach full size, scientists find
articles.summary: Tyrannosaurus rex may have been a much slower grower than scientists realized. A new study of 17 tyrannosaur fossils found that the giant predator likely took about 40 years to reach its full size of roughly eight tons, extending previous estimates by 15 years.
articles.content:
For years, scientists believed Tyrannosaurus rex reached its adult size at around age 25. But a major new study suggests the iconic predator may have taken much longer to mature. According to the research, T. rex likely continued growing for about 40 years before reaching its maximum size of roughly eight tons.

The findings come from an analysis of 17 tyrannosaur fossils spanning a wide range of ages, from young juveniles to massive adults. Researchers say the work provides the most detailed reconstruction yet of how Tyrannosaurus grew throughout its life.

The study was published in the journal PeerJ.

Reading the Growth Rings Hidden Inside Dinosaur Bones

To estimate the age of dinosaurs, paleontologists often examine growth rings preserved inside fossilized bones. These rings are somewhat similar to the annual rings found in tree trunks. Each growth mark can provide clues about how quickly an animal was growing and how old it was when it died.

For decades, scientists have used these rings to reconstruct the life history of Tyrannosaurus rex. However, the new study employed more advanced techniques than earlier investigations. Researchers examined thin slices of fossil bone under specialized lighting that can reveal growth rings that are difficult to detect using standard methods.

The team also used sophisticated statistical models to combine information from multiple specimens. This allowed them to create a more complete picture of growth across the entire lifespan of T. rex.

The results indicate that Tyrannosaurus remained in a growth phase about 15 years longer than previously thought.

In addition, the findings suggest that some fossils traditionally assigned to T. rex may actually belong to other closely related species, or differ for other biological reasons.

Largest T. Rex Dataset Ever Assembled

"This is the largest data set ever assembled for Tyrannosaurus rex," says Holly Woodward, a professor of anatomy at Oklahoma State University who led the research effort. "Examining the growth rings preserved in the fossilized bones allowed us to reconstruct the animals' year-by-year growth histories."

Unlike a tree stump, which preserves rings from an organism's entire life, dinosaur bones provide only a partial record. A cross section of a T. rex leg bone typically preserves information from just the final 10 to 20 years of the animal's life.

To overcome that limitation, the researchers combined growth records from multiple individuals of different ages.

"We came up with a new statistical approach that stitches together growth records from different specimens to estimate the growth trajectory of T. rex across all stages of life in greater detail than any previous study," explains Nathan Myhrvold, a mathematician and paleobiologist at Intellectual Ventures who led the statistical analysis.

"The composite growth curve provides a much more realistic view of how Tyrannosaurus grew and how much they varied in size."

A Slower Path to Becoming a Giant Predator

The new results paint a different picture of Tyrannosaurus development than earlier studies.

Instead of rapidly reaching adulthood, T. rex appears to have grown at a steadier pace over several decades. According to the researchers, this prolonged growth period may have helped younger tyrannosaurs occupy different ecological niches as they matured.

In ecology, a niche refers to the role an organism plays within its environment, including what it eats, where it lives, and how it interacts with other species.

"A four-decade growth phase may have allowed younger tyrannosaurs to fill a variety of ecological roles within their environments," says coauthor Jack Horner of Chapman University. "That could be one factor that allowed them to dominate the end of the Cretaceous Period as apex carnivores."

The Cretaceous Period ended about 66 million years ago, shortly before the extinction of non-avian dinosaurs.

Are Some Famous T. Rex Fossils Actually Different Species?

The study also contributes to an ongoing debate among paleontologists.

Although Tyrannosaurus rex is one of the most famous dinosaurs ever discovered, some researchers have argued that not every fossil labeled as T. rex necessarily belongs to the same species.

One controversial proposal suggests that several smaller fossils represent a separate dinosaur called Nanotyrannus rather than young Tyrannosaurus individuals. Other researchers have proposed that even some large specimens may belong to multiple closely related species.

The issue remains unresolved.

To investigate the question, the researchers included 17 fossils belonging to what they describe as the "Tyrannosaurus rex species complex," a term acknowledging the possibility that more than one species or subspecies could be represented.

Two particularly famous specimens, nicknamed "Jane" and "Petey," stood out from the rest. Their growth patterns differed significantly from those of the other fossils in the study.

While growth data alone cannot determine whether these animals belonged to different species, the unusual patterns make that possibility worthy of further investigation.

The researchers note that a separate recent study by Zanno and Napoli reached a similar conclusion using different methods, classifying Jane and Petey as two distinct species of Nanotyrannus.

Hidden Growth Rings Could Change Dinosaur Research

Another important finding involves the discovery of previously overlooked growth markers inside dinosaur bones.

Woodward, Myhrvold, and Horner found that circularly polarized and cross-polarized light can reveal a new type of growth ring. These hidden features may help explain discrepancies that have puzzled researchers studying dinosaur growth.

Because the approach is supported by strong statistical evidence, it could influence how scientists examine fossils in future studies, not only for T. rex but for many other dinosaur species as well.

"Interpreting multiple closely spaced growth marks is tricky," Myhrvold says. "We found strong evidence that the protocols typically used in growth studies may need to be revised."

A New Look at the Life of Tyrannosaurus Rex

More than a century after Tyrannosaurus rex was first described by scientists, the giant predator continues to reveal new surprises.

By combining a larger fossil sample, improved imaging techniques, and innovative statistical analysis, the new research provides one of the clearest views yet of how T. rex developed from a young dinosaur into one of the largest and most formidable land predators in Earth's history.

The findings suggest that the king of dinosaurs may have taken far longer to grow up than anyone previously realized.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in paleontology using advanced imaging and statistical techniques to better understand T. rex growth, providing new insights into dinosaur biology and evolution. This has broad significance for science and education, offering a clearer picture of a famous species' life history and potentially revising long-held assumptions.
articles.personality_title: New study shows T. rex took 40 years to reach full size
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists long believed that Tyrannosaurus rex, a giant dinosaur, grew to its full size by age 25. This idea was based on looking at growth rings inside dinosaur bones, which are like tree rings showing age and growth.

**What happened** – A recent study examined 17 T. rex fossils of different ages using advanced imaging and statistical methods. These techniques revealed more growth rings than before and combined data from many fossils. The study found that T. rex likely took about 40 years to reach its full size of around eight tons, about 15 years longer than previously thought.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand T. rex’s life. Instead of growing quickly, it grew steadily over decades. This longer growth time might have helped younger T. rex dinosaurs have different roles in their environment as they grew. The study also suggests that some fossils thought to be T. rex might belong to other similar species, which could reshape how scientists classify these dinosaurs.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study more fossils and use these new techniques to learn about other dinosaurs’ growth. They also want to investigate unusual fossils like "Jane" and "Petey" to see if they are different species. This new way of reading growth rings could improve how paleontologists study dinosaur life histories in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Advanced methods reveal that T. rex grew more slowly than thought, taking 40 years to reach full size and reshaping our understanding of this famous dinosaur’s life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude révèle que le T. rex mettait 40 ans à atteindre sa taille adulte
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient depuis longtemps que le Tyrannosaurus rex, un dinosaure géant, atteignait sa taille adulte vers l'âge de 25 ans. Cette idée venait de l’étude des anneaux de croissance dans ses os, similaires aux anneaux des arbres qui indiquent l’âge et la croissance.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude récente a analysé 17 fossiles de T. rex de différents âges en utilisant des techniques d’imagerie avancées et des méthodes statistiques. Ces techniques ont révélé plus d’anneaux de croissance qu’avant et ont combiné les données de plusieurs fossiles. L’étude montre que le T. rex a probablement mis environ 40 ans pour atteindre sa taille complète d’environ huit tonnes, soit 15 ans de plus que ce que l’on pensait.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de la vie du T. rex. Au lieu de grandir rapidement, il grandissait lentement pendant plusieurs décennies. Cette croissance plus longue aurait permis aux jeunes T. rex d’occuper différents rôles dans leur environnement en grandissant. L’étude suggère aussi que certains fossiles attribués au T. rex pourraient appartenir à d’autres espèces proches, ce qui pourrait modifier la classification scientifique de ces dinosaures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’étudier davantage de fossiles et d’appliquer ces nouvelles méthodes pour mieux comprendre la croissance d’autres dinosaures. Ils veulent aussi examiner des fossiles particuliers comme « Jane » et « Petey » pour savoir s’ils appartiennent à des espèces différentes. Cette nouvelle manière d’analyser les anneaux de croissance pourrait améliorer l’étude de l’histoire de vie des dinosaures.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des méthodes avancées montrent que le T. rex grandissait plus lentement que prévu, prenant 40 ans pour atteindre sa taille adulte et modifiant notre compréhension de ce dinosaure célèbre.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio muestra que el T. rex tardó 40 años en alcanzar su tamaño completo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos creyeron que el Tyrannosaurus rex, un dinosaurio gigante, alcanzaba su tamaño adulto alrededor de los 25 años. Esta idea se basaba en el estudio de los anillos de crecimiento en sus huesos, parecidos a los anillos de los árboles que muestran la edad y el crecimiento.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio reciente analizó 17 fósiles de T. rex de diferentes edades usando técnicas avanzadas de imagen y métodos estadísticos. Estas técnicas revelaron más anillos de crecimiento que antes y combinaron datos de varios fósiles. El estudio encontró que el T. rex probablemente tardó unos 40 años en alcanzar su tamaño completo de aproximadamente ocho toneladas, 15 años más de lo que se pensaba.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia cómo entendemos la vida del T. rex. En lugar de crecer rápido, crecía lentamente durante varias décadas. Este tiempo de crecimiento más largo pudo haber ayudado a los T. rex jóvenes a tener diferentes roles en su entorno a medida que crecían. El estudio también sugiere que algunos fósiles considerados T. rex podrían pertenecer a otras especies similares, lo que podría cambiar la clasificación científica de estos dinosaurios.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar más fósiles y usar estas nuevas técnicas para aprender sobre el crecimiento de otros dinosaurios. También quieren investigar fósiles inusuales como "Jane" y "Petey" para ver si son especies diferentes. Esta nueva forma de leer los anillos de crecimiento podría mejorar cómo los paleontólogos estudian la historia de vida de los dinosaurios.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Métodos avanzados revelan que el T. rex crecía más lentamente de lo pensado, tardando 40 años en alcanzar su tamaño completo y cambiando nuestra comprensión de la vida de este famoso dinosaurio.
